full_content:
Statewide Polling in Massachusetts Shows Strong Support for Prohibiting Sales of New Fur 
Products and Concerns About the Fur Farm Industry

Executive Summary

Recent polling of Massachusetts residents indicates strong support for statewide prohibition of the 
sale of new fur products. Initial ﬁndings show 67% of respondents support statewide legislation. 
After respondents engaged with more information about the fur farm industry, support increases to 
73%. Majority support holds across party aBiliation. Respondents’ strongest concerns about the fur 
farm industry varies across party, though animal welfare conditions are a shared concern.

Key Findings

Strong majority support for statewide prohibition of new fur sales.

Key Findings

Strong majority support for statewide prohibition of new fur sales.

The poll asked Massachusetts residents about the following state legislation, once near the 
beginning and again near the end of the survey. This was to measure baseline support for the 
legislation and how that support might change after learning more about the fur farm industry. 
Respondents could indicate whether they would support or oppose it, or if they were unsure.

Currently, there is state legislation that would prohibit the sale of new fur products in 
Massachusetts from animals held in fur factory farms.

Currently, there is state legislation that would prohibit the sale of new fur products in 
Massachusetts from animals held in fur factory farms.

This legislation would have exemptions for: used or secondhand fur, fur used for 
religious purposes, fur from hunted and trapped animals, ﬁshing lures, or fur from 
domestic species typically raised for food production, such as cowhide, shearling, 
and wool.

Baseline support for state legislation was established with about 67% of respondents, which 
increased to 73% later in the survey after reading more information about the fur farm industry. 
Most of the increase in support comes from respondents having shifted away from being unsure.

Baseline majority support and increase in support holds across party lines.

Majority support for this legislation was found across self-identiﬁed party aBiliations. Democrats 
demonstrate the highest support (76% initial to 84% ﬁnal), followed by Independents (63% initial to 
69% ﬁnal), and Republicans (57% to 63% ﬁnal). For Democrats, the shift towards support is largely 
drawn from those previously unsure, while for Republicans this is from those previously opposed, 
and for Independents movement is found from those previously opposed or unsure.

Majorities ﬁnd information about the fur farm industry “very” or “somewhat” concerning.

Respondents were presented with brief information about multiple negative impacts of the fur farm 
industry. For each, they indicated whether they found the information very concerning, somewhat 
concerning, not very concerning, not at all concerning, or if they were unsure.

An overwhelming majority of respondents expressed they are very or somewhat concerned about 
these negative impacts. These are led by harms to the environment from fur processing facilities 
(90%), poor animal welfare conditions on fur factory farms (88%), public health concerns from 
disease outbreaks (88%), and unregulated foreign fur factory production (84%).

When breaking down the results by levels of concern, a strong majority of respondents are very 
concerned about the environment, animal welfare, and public health, with a simple majority being 
very concerned about unregulated foreign production.

• 
Environment (64% very, 26% somewhat, 7% not very/not, 3% unsure) 
• 
Animal welfare (64% very, 24% somewhat, 8% not very/not, 4% unsure) 
• 
Public health (63% very, 24% somewhat, 8% not very/not, 4% unsure) 
• 
Unregulated foreign production (55% very, 29% somewhat, 12% not very/not, 5% unsure)

A strong majority of Democrats are very concerned about each aspect, led by public health (83%), 
the environment (81%), animal welfare (79%), and foreign production (68%). Similarly, most 
Independents are very concerned about the environment (58%), animal welfare (58%), and public 
health (56%). About half of Republicans are very concerned about animal welfare (51%).

Methodology

Methodology

Findings are based on a poll commissioned by FOUR PAWS and conducted by YouGov from 
November 18 – December 1, 2025, among 800 Massachusetts residents. The margin of error for the 
full sample is +/- 4.85; the margin of error for subgroups varies and is higher. Some percentages 
may not add to 100 due to rounding.
